data.name: Sergio Rochet
data.description: Was fairly unknown when he appeared in Danubio’s league title celebrations in 2014. Then the third-choice keeper at 21, Rochet was soon packing his bags for the Netherlands and AZ Alkmaar. In 2019 he was brought back to Uruguay by Nacional to be the back-up to Luis Mejía. “He was on holiday back home, in his house on the water, when he told us that his move to Nacional had been confirmed. It was his dream,” said his mother, Graciela. She could barely believe it herself when he played for Uruguay at the 2022 World Cup. “The day I saw him in the first game was a shock. I stopped in front of the TV and the only thing I did was cry,” she said.

data.name: José María Giménez
data.description: When he was playing in Danubio’s academy at 17, his 14-year-old sister Agustina became pregnant. "Josema", his eyes filled with tears, told his parents that he would provide for the baby. He considered leaving football and finding a job but Danubio helped him to keep playing. At 18, he signed for Atlético Madrid and earned his first international cap, facing Colombia’s Radamel Falcao on his debut. “I would say things to distract him. For example, during an attack, I asked him: ‘Got a car in Madrid?’” Days later, Giménez inked the date of the game on his body. Now with over 100 caps and three World Cups under his belt, the centre-back goes into this summer as captain after the retirement of Diego Godín, his “partner of a thousand battles” and fellow La Liga winner at Atlético.

data.name: Sebastián Cáceres
data.description: Days before the final of the Torneo Intermedio that falls between the two halves of the Uruguayan domestic season, Cáceres had an offer on the table from América in Mexico. Instead of accepting the move and the millions that would set him up for life, he decided to stay at his boyhood club, Liverpool, in Montevideo. The final ended in glory. “I wanted to be a champion with Liverpool. I wanted to play that game for the club that made me,” he said. América came back the following year and the centre-back has won three league titles there. Has played for Uruguay at every level from under-17 upwards, making his senior bow in 2022.

data.name: Ronald Araújo
data.description: Araújo has come through a difficult spell in his life. He made his senior debut for Rentistas aged 16 and has been under the spotlight ever since. Towards the end of last year, after a red card against Chelsea in the Champions League, he decided he needed to step away from the game to receive treatment for depression, which had been affecting him for more than a year already. “We have to understand that aside from being footballers, we are people,” he said when he made his return 47 days later. The centre-back, who was training with Boca Juniors aged 10, made his international debut at 21. He went to the last World Cup but injury prevented him from making an appearance in Qatar. A two-time La Liga champion, he is now part of Barcelona’s captaincy team.

data.name: Manuel Ugarte
data.description: This year is Ugarte’s 25th on this planet and his 10th in professional football. He was a 15-year-old prodigy at Fénix and, in four years in Europe, he went from Famalição in Portugal to Manchester United via Sporting and Paris Saint-Germain. The midfielder made his international bow aged 20, his mother saying: “Manuel called me and his voice was trembling because he was sharing a dressing room with teammates that, until a few days before, he was playing with on the PlayStation.” El Poeta (the Poet), as they call him, has appeared on social media alongside his mum dancing to plena, a genre of music with African-Caribbean roots. On the football pitch his defining characteristic is hard work. “You have to leave it all out there,” he says.

data.name: Rodrigo Bentancur
data.description: A 12-year-old Bentancur left his little inland town of 11,000 people to take the plunge in Buenos Aires. Boca Juniors thought they had found a special talent in the tall and skinny kid. “It was difficult. I didn’t want to know anything about it, but my dad convinced me,” he said. He was part of the Uruguay team that won the Under-20 South American title in 2017 and, by the end of the year, was a full international and a Juventus player. At his first World Cup he set up Edinson Cavani’s winner against Portugal in the last 16. “I celebrated as if I’d scored it myself,” he said. Injury problems have stalled his promising career but the Spurs midfielder is now a senior member of Marcelo Bielsa’s squad. “He has the qualities to play anywhere on the pitch,” the head coach says.

data.name: Nicolás de la Cruz
data.description: De la Cruz was offered a contract by the Montevideo side Liverpool before he’d even kicked a ball in his first training session. His ability and attitude had been obvious from the start. “He loved getting kicked,” said a youth teammate of his. He was as crafty off the pitch as he was on it. In meetings that took place at the training ground he would sit in the chair of Liverpool’s historic president, José Luis Palma, with such impudence that instead of resenting the youngster, Palma could only smile. His biggest idol was his half-brother, the former Uruguay winger Carlos Sánchez, and the pair have both played for River Plate. De la Cruz went to the 2022 World Cup and has since come back from a knee injury that a Flamengo club doctor said he would not recover from. “Never let anyone tell you that you can’t do anything,” he said.

data.name: Federico Valverde
data.description: One of the world’s best had humble beginnings. He doesn’t like admitting it, simply saying his parents “were very hard-working”. He would accompany his mother to her market stall and started playing football so young that he was still wearing nappies. When he arrived at Real Madrid in 2016 feeling like “Cristiano Ronaldo and Messi put together”, he was self-conscious when comparing his clothes with those of his peers. “You’re wearing a €2 T-shirt,” he said to himself. Ironically for a player who used to annoy his youth coaches because he didn’t like running, the midfielder has risen up the ranks at Madrid thanks to his outstanding physical attributes. He scored on debut for Uruguay aged 19 but missed out on the World Cup in 2018, which was a blow. He arrives at this one as his country’s talisman. A hat-trick against Manchester City in the Champions League in March confirmed his elite status. His 2025-26 season ended on a low, however, as he was taken to hospital after an altercation with his Real teammate Aurélien Tchouaméni. In a statement Valverde said: “At no point did my teammate hit me, and I didn’t hit him either.”

data.name: Darwin Núñez
data.description: “One day you’ll see me playing in Europe,” Núñez used to tell his coaches at youth level in Artigas in northern Uruguay. His parents worked overtime to try to provide for him, including buying him shoes to play football in. “I don’t forget where I came from … My mother used to go out to collect bottles on the street that she could sell,” he said. Serious knee injuries as a teenager made Núñez consider quitting football but he chose to fight. A rapid rise took in Peñarol, Almería and Benfica before a big move to Liverpool. In three years at Anfield he won the Premier League and the League Cup. The 26-year-old is now in Saudi Arabia. He spent months without playing in the Pro League after Karim Benzema took his place as a designated foreign player in January. This will be his second World Cup.

data.name: Giorgian de Arrascaeta
data.description: A move to Europe is the ultimate goal for most footballers in South America. Not for De Arrascaeta. The 32-year-old is one of the best players on a continent he has never left. He won two Brazilian cups with Cruzeiro and is now an idol at Flamengo after picking up 20 trophies in seven years in Brazil, including three Copa Libertadores. Despite these feats, he has had his fair share of critics with the national team because he often fails to produce his club form in celeste colours. “Criticism is part and parcel of football and you get used to it with experience,” he says. He scored twice against Ghana in the final group game of the 2022 World Cup but events elsewhere consigned Uruguay to an early exit.

data.name: Facundo Pellistri
data.description: Manchester United paid US$10m to sign an 18-year-old Pellistri from Peñarol in 2020. He has not hit the heights once expected of him, when the former Uruguay international Carlos Aguilera said he had the potential to be “one of the best in the world”. The son of an accountant and a paediatrician whose godfather is a singer in a famous Uruguayan rock band, Pellistri struck the right chords in his first World Cup. He shone as an unexpected starter in Diego Alonso’s team of 2022 but has seen his club career stall in multiple underwhelming loan spells in Spain. Now in Greece on a permanent basis, playing under the now-dismissed Rafa Benítez this past season.

data.name: Santiago Mele
data.description: Mele was all over the Uruguayan front pages when he made two saves in a penalty shootout victory against Portugal at the 2017 Under-20 World Cup. After the game, he revealed his secret: “I was watching them. I wanted to play mind games because I know that has a really big influence.” A move to Europe followed soon after but he played very little during spells in Turkey and Spain. Plaza Colonia offered him a chance to start over and, after spells in Argentina and Colombia, the national team started to take an interest. “Those three years in Turkey were difficult – so was returning to Uruguay to take charge of my career again. Alongside giving myself to God and trusting in him, these are the fundamental reasons why I am where I am,” he said in 2023 after his first senior cap.

data.name: Guillermo Varela
data.description: Has revived his career in Brazil after initially leaving Europe under a cloud. His performances at the Under-20 World Cup in 2013, where Uruguay reached the final, were enough to attract interest from Manchester United. From there, he went on loan to Real Madrid – only playing for the B team – and Eintracht Frankfurt. His time in Germany ended on a sour note because of a swollen tattoo he got shortly before the DFB-Pokal final. “All the other players had tattoos,” he said. “In my case, I didn’t get an infection but the coach said I’d disrespected him because I missed a training session a few days before the final.” After spells in Denmark and Russia, the defender has won two Copa Libertadores titles with Flamengo.

data.name: Agustín Canobbio
data.description: Canobbio has a very particular story. He was a late grower and was left out of national-team age groups despite his obvious talents. The setbacks kept coming: aged 22 he suffered a double fracture of a leg. But he refused to give up and after making his return he said: “A year after my injury I remember what I have been through and the path I travelled and now I value more than ever the opportunity to be able to enjoy this beautiful sport again.” And it showed on the pitch. At 23 he was chosen as the best player in the Uruguayan league and moved to Brazil after which he was given his national-team debut. He is the son of the former Uruguayan striker Osvaldo, and has a motto that inspires him daily: “You have to live for football and then live from football.”

data.name: Emiliano Martínez
data.description: Arrived at Nacional’s academy as a No 10, with Bruno Fornaroli as his idol, and joined the first team as a defensive midfielder, looking up to Sergio Busquets. Martínez would hitch a ride with one of his teammate’s mothers to travel the 130km from Maldonado to Montevideo and back for training each day. He was a Copa Libertadores champion at under-18 level and made his senior bow a year later, winning the Primera División. He kept his cool in a shootout to send Nacional into the 2020 Copa Libertadores quarter-finals, admitting: “I’d never taken a penalty, not even in training.” An impressive spell at Midtjylland in Denmark opened the doors to the national team, where played with Luis Suárez. “It was a dream for me – to share a dressing room with him,” Martínez said.

data.name: Mathías Olivera
data.description: Although he has made his name as a left-back, Marcelo Bielsa deploys him as a centre-back. “He’s quite a slight centre-back who is comfortable in wide positions and I like this type of centre-back with a past life as a full-back,” the head coach said in 2024. Olivera, the son of teenage parents, stood out from a young age because of his natural speed, power and ball-striking. His first steps in Europe were hampered by homesickness. He passed a medical at Galatasaray aged 19 but did not sign a contract, joining Getafe in Spain instead. “I really missed home. I cried every day and wanted to go back but obviously I couldn’t.” He became a mainstay under José Bordalás and made his Uruguay debut in 2019. Since playing at the 2022 World Cup – “a dream” – he has won two Serie A titles with Napoli.

data.name: Matías Viña
data.description: Resilience is one of his biggest strengths. When on the verge of signing his first pro contract with Nacional, the defender broke his collarbone. The club paused the deal but waited for him to complete his recovery. He competed for the left-back place during his academy days with Mathías Olivera, his competition in the Uruguay squad now. He struggled to establish himself in the Nacional first team as a result, sometimes playing at centre-back but often not at all, and didn’t become a regular until the age of 22. “It got me down at times because I wasn’t playing but my family and friends helped me a lot. They kept telling me my time would come,” he said. In 2019 he made his international debut and kickstarted his club career. He’s a three-time Copa Libertadores winner and was signed by José Mourinho at Roma, where he had a loan spell at Bournemouth.

data.name: Brian Rodríguez
data.description: Rodríguez left his home town of Tranqueras, population 7,000, on the northern border with Brazil to join the youth ranks of Tacuarembó, landing a trial at Peñarol at the age of 15. The first training session was tough. “They kicked him and stole the ball off him, but he put up with it well,” remembered his coach, José Perdomo. Rodríguez’s conduct cost him at times. He was made to train with younger age groups twice because of bad behaviour but made his first-team debut at Peñarol in 2018. “He recognises and learns from his mistakes,” said Alejandro Valenzuela, his personal coach from a young age. When playing in MLS with LA FC aged 19 he was given a first senior Uruguay call-up. Has won three league titles in Mexico. “Those who know me know what it’s taken for me to get here,” he says.

data.name: Rodrigo Aguirre
data.description: Has been through the ringer during his career. At the Under-17 World Cup in 2011 the striker was knocked out in the final against Mexico and woke up in hospital. “The first thing I remember was when the doctors asked me what my name was and what day I was born,” he said. Two years later he missed the Under-20 World Cup owing to a heart problem. Aguirre is well travelled but it wasn’t until a purple patch at América in Mexico that he caught Marcelo Bielsa’s eye. He made his full international debut aged 30 and scored against Colombia in a World Cup qualifier at Montevideo's Estadio Centenario. “I couldn’t have dreamed it better,” he said.

data.name: Maximiliano Araújo
data.description: In 2012 a video went viral in Uruguay showing a small child with the No 10 on his back running down the left wing and setting up a goal in a youth football match. This was Maximiliano Araújo crossing the ball for his brother, César, to win the prestigious Danone Cup. The video shows César holding back tears after some wild celebrations. Both boys are now professional footballers and have shared a dressing room at international level. “Maxi” is still running down the left wing. Marcelo Bielsa was once asked to describe the Sporting player. “I think Maxi’s a terrible player who got where he is because of luck. He won’t get any further. I’ve never seen him play – he was recommended to me.” It was a joke, though no one got it. “I like players who cause problems, who run directly from one box to the other, who get lots of crosses in and Maxi has all those traits,” he clarified.

data.name: Federico Viñas
data.description: “I’m Federico Viñas, it’s possible they confused me with Matías Viña,” the striker said when he was called up by Uruguay for the first time – he couldn’t quite believe it. Playing international football is a far cry from the Casabó neighbourhood in Montevideo where Viñas earned a living delivering meat produce. One day he went to try out for the amateur team Mar de Fondo. From there he went to Juventud in Las Piedras. The striker, nicknamed El Tanque (the Tank), arrived 15kg overweight but made his first-team debut a year later in Uruguay’s second tier. Interest from América in Mexico was almost as unexpected as the international call-up that followed a few years later. Has spent the last two seasons on loan at Real Oviedo, winning promotion to La Liga in the first.

data.name: Joaquín Piquerez
data.description: Nine months after making his senior debut for Defensor Sporting, a 19-year-old Piquerez stepped up to take a penalty in the Uruguayan championship final against Peñarol and missed, Defensor losing the shootout 4-2. Two years later, when he was 21, he was released. “I grew up at Defensor and this isn’t the way I wanted to leave,” he said. He could have fallen by the wayside but he used that setback as fuel. Six months at Uruguay’s River Plate opened the door to Peñarol and his knack of scoring from distance led to Palmeiras coming calling. In 2021 Piquerez went full circle, lifting the Copa Libertadores at Montevideo’s Estadio Centenario, where he had missed that penalty four years earlier. Has become a regular under Marcelo Bielsa but picked up an ankle injury in March’s friendly draw with England.

data.name: Fernando Muslera
data.description: Muslera was born in Buenos Aires on the day a Maradona-inspired Argentina beat Uruguay in the last 16 at the 1986 World Cup, but there was never any doubt about which country would win the knockout tie for his allegiance – he was named after the Uruguayan striker Fernando Morena, and his family moved to Uruguay when he was an infant. “My passport says that I have Argentinian nationality, but I feel 100% Uruguayan,” he says. His life changed one day as an eight-year-old, already a keen footballer and prolific goalscorer, when his team’s goalkeeper failed to turn up for a match. “Like any kid I just wanted to play,” he recalled. “I didn’t care what position – though back then I didn’t like playing in goal at all.” It would be fair to say he was not temperamentally suited to the position. “Whenever I conceded a goal, I would start crying,” he said. “I didn’t want my team to lose. I took it very personally, as if it was my fault.” Now he is making history, as the first Uruguayan to feature in five World Cups.

data.name: Santiago Bueno
data.description: In the middle of the Under-20 World Cup in 2017, while he was starting at centre-back for Uruguay, Bueno signed a contract with Barcelona. It was a massive jump for a player who still hadn’t made a senior appearance for Peñarol in the Primera División. He arrived in Catalonia as an under-20 South American champion but struggled to gain a foothold in the European game. Two years later, he left Barcelona as a free agent and joined Girona. “I had to take a step back in order to take two steps forward,” he said. The gamble paid off and within a couple of years Bueno was playing in La Liga. A move to the Premier League with Wolves followed and he has been dreaming “every day” for a spot on the plane to the World Cup.

data.name: Juan Manuel Sanabria
data.description: After winning the Under-20 Copa Libertadores with Nacional, an 18-year-old Sanabria signed for Atlético Madrid. It was a big change for a kid who used to travel the 100km to training and back in Montevideo so he could “chill out at home” in Florida during his time in the Nacional academy. In a country such as Uruguay, that sort of daily commute is an immense undertaking. Sanabria played for Atlético’s B team before leaving for their feeder club in Mexico, Atlético San Luis, where he swapped midfield for left-back. “He’s technically excellent. No one has more rhythm than him and he can play as a full-back, in central midfield or as a winger,” said Marcelo Bielsa, who called him up for the first time last year.

data.name: Rodrigo Zalazar
data.description: José Zalazar was one of a number of 1980s Uruguayan footballers who started the trend of upping sticks for Europe. Central to his game was his powerful shooting, on display in Spain for more than 10 years. Rodrigo was born in the city of Albacete, whose club his father made over 200 appearances for. Zalazar Jr's youth career was spent in Spain before spells at senior level in Germany and Poland. Unlike his brother Kuki, who played at youth level for Spain, Rodrigo’s national allegiance was never in doubt. “I never thought about playing for Spain. I always dreamed of playing for Uruguay,” Zalazar said when he arrived in Montevideo after his first call-up in Bielsa’s first squad in 2023. Since scoring twice on his debut against Nicaragua, international minutes have been few and far between.
